title-group.article-title: Comment on ‘Worldwide prevalence of haemorrhoids: a systematic review and meta-analysis’

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Proctology, Beilun People’s Hospital, Ningbo, China
bDepartment of Proctology, Beilun Branch of the First Affiliated Hospital, College of Medicine, Zhejiang University, Ningbo, China

event.event-desc: published online in a building issue

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Published by Informa UK Limited, trading as Taylor & Francis Group

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

body.p: We read with great interest the systematic review and meta-analysis by Esmaeilnia Shirvani et al. [1] which provides a comprehensive synthesis of global haemorrhoidal disease (HD) prevalence. The authors’ efforts in integrating data from 150 studies across 45 countries are commendable and offer a valuable epidemiological overview. However, we wish to raise several methodological and interpretative points that, if addressed, could further enhance the robustness and clinical applicability of the findings.

The authors report extreme heterogeneity across all prevalence estimates (I2 = 100.0% for point prevalence). While common in prevalence meta-analyses, an I2 of 100% necessitates a cautious interpretation of the pooled estimate (25.92%) as a ‘global average.’ It indicates that the variance between studies is as large as the variance within studies, suggesting the pooled figure may not represent any single population reliably. The authors conducted extensive subgroup and meta-regression analyses, which only explained 16.86% of the between-study variance. This low explanatory power, as discussed by Borenstein et al. in the context of meta-analysis [2], suggests that critical, unmeasured effect modifiers (e.g. detailed dietary patterns, exact diagnostic criteria variability, genetic factors) are driving the differences. We recommend the authors more strongly emphasize that their pooled estimate is a descriptive statistical summary of highly disparate data, and that the presented subgroup results (e.g. by region) should be interpreted as hypothesis-generating rather than definitive comparisons.

Table 4 presents risk factors using unadjusted estimates, such as family history (OR = 4.19). Per GRADE guidance, unadjusted associations from observational studies constitute low-quality evidence due to confounding. Factors like age, obesity, and socioeconomic status may substantially inflate these associations. We recommend clearly labeling these as ‘unadjusted associations’ and stating they identify candidates for causal evaluation via longitudinal or Mendelian randomization studies (e.g. Huang et al. on adiposity) [3], not as confirmed causal risks.

The reported 1.22% annual increase in prevalence attributed to lifestyle changes is plausibly confounded by ‘diagnostic intensification.’ Increased endoscopic screening over recent decades (e.g. Peery et al.) detects more asymptomatic cases [4]. The authors’ own subgroup analysis confirms higher prevalence with invasive (28.05%) vs. non-invasive methods (23.03%). We suggest acknowledging this key alternative explanation and, if feasible, presenting a sensitivity analysis of the temporal trend stratified by diagnostic method.

In conclusion, this study represents a significant effort to map the global burden of haemorrhoidal disease. Addressing these points in a revision would strengthen the methodological transparency and clinical interpretation of the work, ensuring its findings are applied appropriately in both research and public health contexts. We thank the authors for their contribution and look forward to their response.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: No potential conflicts of interest relevant to this article were reported.

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: A data statement is not applicable to this article, as no new data were generated in this study.

ref.mixed-citation: Esmaeilnia Shirvani A, Pakdaman K, Maleki Z, et al. Worldwide prevalence of haemorrhoids: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Ann Med. 2026;58(1):2606433. doi:10.1080/07853890.2025.2606433.41450176 PMC12777808
Borenstein M, Hedges LV, Higgins JPT, et al. A basic introduction to fixed-effect and random-effects models for meta-analysis. Res Synth Methods. 2010;1(2):97–111. doi:10.1002/jrsm.12.26061376

Huang J, Gui Y, Qin H, et al. Causal association between adiposity and hemorrhoids: a Mendelian randomization study. Front Med (Lausanne). 2023;10:1229925. doi:10.3389/fmed.2023.1229925.37869154 PMC10587414
Peery AF, Sandler RS, Galanko JA, et al. Risk factors for hemorrhoids on screening colonoscopy. PLoS One. 2015;10(9):e0139100. doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0139100.26406337 PMC4583402
